questions.question: What is the primary purpose of swapping in an operating system?
keyPoints: To free up main memory (RAM) by moving inactive processes or parts of processes to secondary storage.
Allows the system to run more processes than could otherwise fit in physical memory simultaneously.
Enables a form of memory management that extends the logical address space available to processes.

questions.question: Describe the key steps involved in swapping a process out of main memory and back in.
keyPoints: The operating system identifies an inactive process or a process that has been waiting for a long time.
It copies the entire process's address space (text, data, stack segments) from RAM to a dedicated area on the disk (the swap space).
The memory frames occupied by the process are then marked as free and can be reallocated to other processes.
When the swapped-out process needs to run again, the scheduler requests it to be brought back in.
The OS allocates free memory frames and copies the process's data from the swap space on the disk back into RAM.

questions.question: Compare and contrast swapping with paging. Discuss the major performance considerations and challenges inherent to the swapping technique.
keyPoints: Scope: Swapping moves entire processes, while paging moves fixed-size blocks (pages) of a process's address space.
Granularity: Paging is more granular, allowing only the necessary inactive parts of a process to be moved, whereas swapping is an all-or-nothing operation for a process.
Performance Challenge: The major performance cost is the high I/O overhead of transferring a large contiguous block (the entire process) to and from the slow disk.
Swap Time: The time required to swap a process is directly proportional to the amount of memory it uses, making it inefficient for large processes.
Context Switch Impact: A context switch to a swapped-out process incurs a very long delay due to the need to load it from disk first, which can severely degrade system responsiveness.
